title: Why Version Control Matters
summary: Open with a standalone story about a project losing its history, then distinguish Git from duplicate final folders and explain the safe local course workspace.

sketchBlocks.title: The Importance of Project History
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown: Imagine working on a large project with multiple collaborators. Each person saves their work in separate folders labeled 'final', 'final_v2', 'final_v3', and so on. Over time, it becomes nearly impossible to track changes, understand why certain decisions were made, or revert to a previous state if something goes wrong. This is where version control systems like Git come into play. They provide a structured way to manage changes, track history, and collaborate effectively without the chaos of multiple 'final' folders.

sketchBlocks.title: Worked Example: How Git Solves Project Chaos
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown: Worked example: Consider a project where a team is developing a new software application. Initially, they save their work in a folder named 'Project'. As changes are made, they create copies like 'Project_final', 'Project_final_v2', and so on. One day, a team member accidentally overwrites a crucial file. Without a version control system, recovering the original file is difficult. However, with Git, every change is recorded as a snapshot. If a mistake occurs, the team can easily revert to a previous version, ensuring no work is lost. This reliable history contrasts sharply with the ambiguous nature of final-copy folder names.

quizDraft.title: Purpose of Version Control
quizDraft.prompt: What is the primary purpose of a version control system like Git?
options: To manage changes and track project history
To increase the speed of development
To replace the need for project management tools
quizDraft.hint: Think about how version control helps manage changes.

help.concept: Version control systems help manage changes to projects over time.
help.hint_1: Consider how version control tracks changes.
help.hint_2: Version control allows you to revert to previous states.

quizDraft.title: Git vs. GitHub
quizDraft.prompt: How does Git differ from GitHub?
options: Git is a local version control system, while GitHub is a hosted platform for collaboration
Git and GitHub are the same
GitHub is a version control system, while Git is a platform for collaboration
Git is used for managing code, while GitHub is used for managing documents
quizDraft.hint: Consider the role of GitHub in relation to Git.

help.concept: Git is a local version control system, while GitHub is a hosted platform for collaboration.
help.hint_1: Git is used locally, while GitHub is online.

quizDraft.title: Benefits of Using Git
quizDraft.prompt: Which of the following are benefits of using Git? Select all that apply.
options: Tracks changes to files over time
Allows multiple people to work on the same project
quizDraft.hint: Think about how Git helps in managing project changes and collaboration.

help.concept: Git provides a structured way to manage changes, track history, and collaborate.
help.hint_1: Consider how Git helps track changes and manage history.
help.hint_2: Git allows multiple people to work on the same project efficiently.

quizDraft.title: Steps in a Git Workflow
quizDraft.prompt: Arrange the steps in a typical Git workflow in the correct order.
quizDraft.hint: Think about the sequence of actions from making changes to committing them.

help.concept: A typical Git workflow involves making changes, staging them, and then committing.
help.hint_1: Consider what happens after you make changes to files.

quizDraft.title: Git Command for Initialization
quizDraft.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the command used to initialize a new Git repository.
quizDraft.template: The command to initialize a new Git repository is '[blank1]'.
quizDraft.hint: This command sets up a new repository in the current directory.

help.concept: The command to initialize a new Git repository is 'the missing term'.

quizDraft.title: Git Status Command
quizDraft.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the command that checks the status of your Git repository.
quizDraft.template: The command to check the status of a Git repository is '[blank1]'.
quizDraft.hint: This command shows the current state of the working directory and staging area.

help.concept: The command to check the status of a Git repository is 'the missing term'.
